_schema._note: Documents council-level spending and fiscal decisions made during the documented fiscal crisis period (FY2025 onward, when City Manager language explicitly called the structural deficit 'not sustainable'). These are collective actions — unanimous or near-unanimous votes with no individual member as the primary driver. Complements incidents.json (individual member behavior) and audit_findings.json (staff/auditor findings). Used to build the council-as-institution accountability record.

categories.fund_mission_creep: Designated fund spending redirected to a purpose outside the fund's voter-approved or statutory mandate
categories.discretionary_addition: New program, facility, or ongoing obligation approved during the fiscal crisis period without offsetting reduction
categories.revenue_base_reduction: Action that removes property from the tax rolls or otherwise reduces the structural revenue base
categories.deferred_liability: Decision that pushes a known cost into a future budget cycle without a funding plan

evidence_tiers.A: Direct documentation — staff report, ordinance text, or meeting minutes with specific dollar amounts and fund designations
evidence_tiers.B: Strong inference from agenda item title, recommendation language, and financial figures
evidence_tiers.C: Pattern-based — inferred from context without a single dispositive document

_schema._fiscal_crisis_note: The FY2025-2026 budget was adopted June 25 2024 with City Manager language explicitly calling the structural deficit 'not sustainable.' Actions after this date are evaluated against that documented context.

actions.title: African American Holistic Resource Center (AAHRC) groundbreaking — $15.1M capital, $1.5–2M/year ongoing
actions.site: 1890 Alcatraz Avenue — existing city-owned building demolished, new 6,000 sq ft facility constructed
actions.property_ownership: City-owned land; property already off tax rolls. No additional tax base reduction, but city retains all capital and operating obligations.
actions.cost_escalation: Original 2022 budget was $7.25M for renovation of existing building. Council voted January 2023 to demolish and build new. Final budget more than doubled to $15.1M — during the fiscal crisis period.
actions.measure_t1_note: Measure T1 was approved by voters in November 2016 with 86.5% support to 'repair, renovate, replace or reconstruct the City's aging existing infrastructure, including facilities, streets, sidewalks, storm drains, and parks.' $13.85M of T1 funds are being used for a new community services facility. Whether a new programmatic facility constitutes 'repair or replacement of aging infrastructure' under the bond's mandate is at minimum arguable.
actions.significance: The AAHRC is a $15.1M capital project — more than double its original $7.25M budget — approved and built during a documented structural fiscal crisis. $13.85M comes from Measure T1, a bond sold to voters as infrastructure repair; a new programmatic community facility is a stretch of that mandate. The $250K General Fund contribution is the smallest line in the capital stack, but the city bears 100% of the ongoing operating obligation ($1.5–2M/year per the 2019 feasibility study) with no identified funding source. The staff report accepting the HUD grant lists 'Alternative Actions Considered: None' — the council had no presented option to pause, scale back, or redirect funds to P1 infrastructure during a fiscal crisis. The project was promoted in the Mayor's February 2026 newsletter as a community achievement with zero mention of cost, operating obligation, or structural deficit context.
actions.notes: The AAHRC is separate from the MLK Jr. Youth Services Center ($23.7M contract, 2025-06-03) — two major new community facilities built simultaneously during the fiscal crisis. Total new community facility capital across both projects: ~$38.8M.

actions.title: Measure M street funds redirected to North Berkeley BART affordable housing predevelopment
actions.fund_source: Measure M — Street and Watershed Improvement Fund
actions.fund_source_mandate: Voter-approved measure for street and watershed improvements
actions.redirected_to: Affordable housing development at North Berkeley BART station
actions.meeting_item: 2025-05-20 Item 2 — Amendment: FY 2025 Annual Appropriations Ordinance (AAO#2)
actions.vote: Ayes: Kesarwani, Taplin, Tregub, O'Keefe, Blackaby, Lunaparra, Humbert, Ishii. Absent: Bartlett.
actions.significance: Berkeley's streets PCI sits 13 points below the city's own target, with a documented $1.8B infrastructure backlog. Measure M was approved by voters to address street and watershed needs. Redirecting $24.5M of Measure M funds to housing predevelopment at BART — a project with its own dedicated housing funds (Measure O, Affordable Housing Mitigation Fee, state grants) — depletes a designated street repair pool while the underlying P1 infrastructure problem compounds. The redirection was bundled into a consent calendar appropriations amendment with no standalone vote or public deliberation on the fund transfer specifically.
actions.notes: The same AAO#2 also includes $3M from Affordable Housing Mitigation Fee and $320K from Measure U1 for North Berkeley BART predevelopment — suggesting multiple fund sources are being layered onto this single project simultaneously.
